desc: The most commonly encountered sphinx moth. Hovers at flowers like a hummingbird at dusk. Caterpillar is the familiar "hornworm" of gardens.

fm.Upperside: Olive-brown FW with white diagonal stripe; HW pink with black bands
fm.Behavior: Hovers at flowers at dusk; strong swift flight
fm.Flight: Mar–Nov; multiple broods; mass migrations some years
fm.Hosts: Extremely polyphagous — evening primrose, portulaca, four o'clock, grape
fm.Habitat: Gardens, desert, roadsides — any habitat during migration

hp: Many families inc. Onagraceae (Oenothera), Vitaceae (Vitis), Portulacaceae

desc: Huge — one of the largest moths in LA. Larva is the familiar tomato/tobacco hornworm of gardens.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown with wavy dark lines; 6 orange lateral abdominal spots (vs. 5 in M. quinquemaculatus)
fm.Behavior: Strong nocturnal flight; visits tubular flowers

desc: Gray sphinx with elegant wavy lines. Associated with chaparral.

fm.Upperside: Smooth gray with fine dark streaks
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; attracted to lights

desc: Large pink-and-brown sphinx. Larvae on grape vines — the "grape hornworm."

fm.Upperside: FW brown with dark patches; HW pink
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; rapid flight

desc: Day-flying bumblebee mimic with clear wings. Hovers at flowers.

fm.Upperside: Clear wings with dark borders; bumblebee-like fuzzy body
fm.Behavior: Diurnal — hovers like hummingbird at flowers

desc: Similar to Snowberry but with red-brown thorax. Day-flying. Rare in LA.

fm.Upperside: Clear wings; red-brown thorax; olive abdomen
fm.Behavior: Diurnal hummingbird mimic
fm.Habitat: Woodland edges; rare in LA County

desc: Giant silk moth with enormous hindwing eyespots. Non-feeding adult — lives only a few days.

fm.Upperside: Tan-brown with large blue-purple-yellow eyespots on HW; transparent FW window
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; attracted to lights; adults do not feed (no functional mouthparts)
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, riparian, urban with host trees

desc: Large reddish silk moth with crescent-shaped wing marks. Western replacement for Cecropia.

fm.Upperside: Reddish-brown with white crescent marks and red-white marginal bands
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; adults non-feeding; ♂ detects ♀ pheromone miles away

desc: Stunning day-flying moth. Cream with intricate black line pattern above; red-orange HW with black spots.

fm.Upperside: FW cream with bold black network lines; HW red-orange with black spots
fm.Behavior: Diurnal slow flier; conspicuous; warning coloration

desc: Day-flying tiger moth of mountain chaparral. Highly polymorphic — dark FW with white spots; HW orange/red with black. Multiple color forms. Transverse Ranges populations.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown FW with cream spots; HW orange with dark spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; warning coloration

desc: Adult is plain orange-buff. Famous for the "woolly bear" caterpillar — the banded brown-and-black larvae.

fm.Upperside: Plain orange-buff with small dark spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; attracted to lights

hp: Polyphagous: plantain, dandelion, nettle, clover, many herbs

desc: Black FW with cream stripes; red HW with black spots. Spectacular pattern.

fm.Upperside: Black FW with cream/white stripes; HW red-orange with black spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; clicks ultrasonically to jam bat sonar

desc: Huge eyespots on hindwings revealed when threatened. ♂ yellow; ♀ red-brown. Stinging caterpillar.

fm.Upperside: ♂: yellow FW, huge blue-black eyespot on yellow HW. ♀: red-brown with same eyespot.
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; flashes eyespots when disturbed; adults non-feeding; caterpillar stings

hp: Polyphagous: willows, maples, oaks, hackberry, birch

desc: Tiny white moth in obligate mutualism with Yucca. Only pollinator of Yucca flowers.

fm.Upperside: Pure white; narrow wings
fm.Behavior: Deliberately pollinates yucca while ovipositing; obligate mutualism

hp: Agavaceae: Yucca spp. — obligate mutualist pollinator

desc: Mottled gray-brown with distinctive silvery figure-8 mark on FW. Abundant garden pest.

fm.Upperside: Mottled gray-brown; silver figure-8 on FW
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; strong flier; attracted to lights

hp: Brassicaceae, Fabaceae, Solanaceae — highly polyphagous crop pest

desc: Small gray moth. Larvae are major agricultural pest — army-like mass migrations.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown with small pale kidney-shaped mark
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; mass flights to lights
fm.Hosts: Extremely polyphagous — beets, lettuce, cotton, ornamentals

hp: Highly polyphagous: beet, alfalfa, cotton, corn, many vegetables

desc: Large Noctuid. Plain brown FW; hindwing bright orange-yellow with black border. Introduced — now abundant.

fm.Upperside: Brown FW; orange-yellow HW with dark border
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hides in soil by day; strong flier

desc: Tiny bright green "emerald" moth. Attaches flower petals to body as camouflage.

fm.Upperside: Bright emerald green with faint white lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; caterpillar decorates itself with flower parts for camo

desc: ♂ brown, flies. ♀ wingless, lays eggs on cocoon. Tussock caterpillar — colorful with hair tufts.

fm.Upperside: ♂: gray-brown with faint darker bands. ♀: wingless, plump.
fm.Behavior: ♂ flies to lights; ♀ never flies; eggs in mass on cocoon; caterpillar showy

hp: Polyphagous: oaks, willows, lupines, many shrubs and trees

desc: Large gray sphinx associated with stone fruits and olives.

fm.Upperside: Gray with dark streaks and pale zigzag lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful flight; hovers at flowers

desc: Large green-and-pink sphinx. Stunning coloration.

fm.Upperside: Olive-green with dark patches and pink highlights
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; tubular flowers

desc: Day-flying silk moth. Black-and-yellow or pink-and-yellow banding. Stinging caterpillar.

fm.Upperside: Variable: yellow/black or rose-pink/black banded pattern; HW eyespots
fm.Behavior: Diurnal — unusual for Saturniidae; fast buzzy flight; caterpillar stings

desc: The worm in the apple. Small gray moth; one of the world's most significant fruit pests.

fm.Upperside: Gray with copper-bronze patch at FW tip
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; ♀ lays eggs on developing fruit; larva bores into apple/pear

desc: Small metallic blue-black day-flying moth. Larvae gregarious on grape leaves, skeletonizing them.

fm.Upperside: Metallic blue-black; orange collar
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; slow buzzy flight; mildly toxic (cyanogenic)

desc: Spectacular large green silk moth. Historically documented in SoCal but now extirpated from the region. Included for ecological/historical context.

fm.Upperside: Pale green with long HW tails; eyespots on each wing
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; adults non-feeding; dramatic long tails

desc: Green larvae are major pest on ornamental flowers (petunias, geraniums). Moth is olive-tan.

fm.Upperside: Olive-tan with pale bands
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; larvae bore into flower buds

desc: Mountain conifer pest. ♂ gray; ♀ wingless. Caterpillar showy with hair tufts. Periodic outbreaks.

fm.Upperside: ♂: gray-brown. ♀: wingless, plump.
fm.Behavior: ♂ flies; ♀ never flies; eggs on cocoon; outbreak species

desc: Day-flying silk moth. Black and white banded. Stinging caterpillar. SoCal buckwheat scrub.

fm.Upperside: Black with white median band and red-orange HW base
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; fast buzzy flight; caterpillar stings painfully

hp: Various chaparral shrubs inc. Ceanothus, Cercocarpus

desc: Day-flying black moth with bold white stripe. Distinctive and easily identified. Clematis specialist.

fm.Upperside: Black with bold white transverse stripe
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; rests with wings spread flat; distinctive pattern

desc: Invasive from South America (via biocontrol gone wrong). Threatens native Opuntia. Larvae bore into cactus pads. Advancing westward.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown; cryptic
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae bore inside Opuntia pads, hollowing them out

desc: Common household pest moth. Larvae in stored grain, flour, cereal.

fm.Upperside: FW with dark and pale banded pattern; olive and brown
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; found in kitchens, pantries, grain storage
fm.Hosts: Stored grain, flour, cereals, dried food

desc: Day-flying black moth with 8 cream spots and orange leg tufts. Grape specialist. Striking.

fm.Upperside: Black with 2 cream-yellow FW spots + 2 HW spots = 8 total
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits flowers; distinctive pattern; orange leg tufts

desc: Remarkable hornet mimic — clear wings, yellow-banded body. Larvae bore in poplar/willow trunks.

fm.Upperside: Clear wings; yellow and dark brown banded body — hornet mimic
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; convincing hornet mimicry; wasp-like buzzy flight

desc: Huge sphinx with pink abdominal spots. Tropical migrant reaching SoCal in warm years. Sweet potato specialist.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown FW; pink-banded abdomen with dark dorsal stripe
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful migrant; arrives from tropics

desc: The largest moth in the world by wingspan (up to 12 in). Extremely rare tropical vagrant to SoCal. A few historical records only.

fm.Upperside: White/gray with complex wavy dark lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; tropical; extraordinarily rare vagrant
fm.Habitat: Historical vagrant records only; tropical species

desc: Large white moth with black spots and orange hindwings. "Woolly bear" caterpillar. 950 LA obs

fm.Pattern: White FW with black spots; HW orange (♂) or white (♀)
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; caterpillar is classic "woolly bear"

hp: Polyphagous: many herbaceous plants, corn, soybean, cotton

desc: Aquatic moth — larvae live underwater in silk shelters on stream rocks. Adults small, triangular.

fm.Pattern: Brown/tan triangular; dark median band
fm.Behavior: Adults near streams; larvae aquatic — silk tubes on submerged rocks

desc: Small orange-and-brown micro moth on sage and mint. Day-active.

fm.Pattern: Orange with brown margins and spots
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits flowers of Lamiaceae

desc: Dull brown cutworm moth. Larvae cut seedlings at soil line — major agricultural pest.

fm.Pattern: Dull brown-gray; dark kidney/orbicular spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; larvae are "cutworms" in soil

desc: Orange-and-brown pyralid. Larvae on Genista, Texas Mountain Laurel, lupines — gregarious, conspicuous.

fm.Pattern: Orange-tan FW; brown HW with pale fringe
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae gregarious, obvious on ornamental Genista

desc: Tiny orange-and-brown moth common on native sages. Day-flying.

fm.Pattern: Orange with brown margins
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; flutters around sage flowers

desc: Purple-rose and yellow micro moth. Strikingly colorful for its size.

fm.Pattern: Rose-purple with yellow spots and margins
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits mint family flowers

desc: Small dark geometrid. Larvae feed on Asteraceae. Common at lights.

fm.Pattern: Dark gray-brown with pale transverse lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; common at porch lights

desc: Small moth on milkweed/swan plant. Increasing with ornamental milkweed plantings.

fm.Pattern: Gray-brown; triangular at rest
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae in milkweed flowers/pods

desc: Plume moth on sage. Wings split into feathery plumes. T-shape at rest.

fm.Pattern: Gray-tan; wings split into T-shape plumes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; distinctive T-shape rest posture

desc: Silver "figure-8" on FW diagnostic. Important crop pest.

fm.Pattern: Mottled brown/gray; silver "U" or figure-8 mark on FW
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; strong flier; to lights and sugar baits

hp: Polyphagous: alfalfa, clover, many crops — looper

desc: Tiny golden-tan geometrid. Larvae on oak.

fm.Pattern: Golden-tan with faint darker lines and central dot
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, urban areas with oaks

desc: Orange-and-brown pyralid on sage scrub.

fm.Pattern: Orange with brown border markings
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; flutters near sage

desc: Pale tan with central FW dot. Larvae migrate en masse — "armyworms."

fm.Pattern: Pale tan-brown; single white FW dot
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae migrate in armies devastating turf/crops

desc: Large geometrid. Larvae eat nearly everything — one of the most polyphagous moth larvae.

fm.Pattern: Straw-yellow with brown zigzag lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae are loopers (inch along) on trees and shrubs
fm.Hosts: Extremely polyphagous — avocado, citrus, roses, oaks

desc: Small crambid pest of Brassicaceae.

fm.Pattern: Gray-tan with darker streaks
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae web leaves of cabbage family

desc: Silver bilobed mark on FW. Related to cabbage looper.

fm.Pattern: Mottled brown/gray; silver bilobed spot on FW
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights

desc: Brown cutworm moth with pale kidney mark. Major garden pest.

fm.Pattern: Brown-gray; pale reniform and orbicular spots; variable
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae are climbing cutworms

desc: Small tortricid. Larvae roll leaves.

fm.Pattern: Tan-brown with darker diagonal markings
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae tie/roll leaves

desc: The most common pantry pest moth. Distinctive two-toned FW — copper outer half, pale inner.

fm.Pattern: FW bicolored — inner half cream/gray, outer half copper-red (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Found in kitchens/pantries; larvae in stored food
fm.Hosts: Stored grain, cereal, dried fruit, nuts, pet food

hp: Stored products: cereals, nuts, dried fruit, chocolate, pet food

desc: Small olive-brown noctuid. Larvae on Oxalis (wood sorrel).

fm.Pattern: Olive-brown with pale transverse lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights

desc: Crambid on Solanaceae.

fm.Pattern: White/cream with brown spots and lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae roll eggplant/Solanum leaves

desc: Brown noctuid. Larvae with distinctive yellow stripe. Agricultural pest.

fm.Pattern: Brown-gray with paler orbicular and reniform spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae gregarious on crops

desc: Huge — up to 6 in wingspan. Dark brown/purple. Tropical migrant. Folklore: omen of death in Latin American cultures.

fm.Pattern: Dark brown-purple with eyespots; bat-like in flight
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful migrant; rests on buildings; folklore associations
fm.Habitat: Urban — rests on building walls during migration

desc: Large gray sphinx with black dashes on FW. Larvae on ash and lilac.

fm.Pattern: Gray with black dashes on FW; black-and-white banded abdomen
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful hovering flight; to lights

desc: Day-flying bumblebee mimic with clear wings. Western replacement for H. diffinis.

fm.Pattern: Clear wings with dark borders; olive/black fuzzy body
fm.Behavior: Diurnal — hovers at flowers like hummingbird

desc: Large pinkish-gray sphinx. Blue eyespots on HW flashed when threatened.

fm.Pattern: Pink-gray FW; HW with blue-black eyespot on pink ground
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; when threatened, flashes HW eyespots

desc: Large erebid associated with fan palms. Larvae feed inside palm flower clusters.

fm.Pattern: Tan-brown; dark median line; elongated wings
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; found near Washingtonia palms
fm.Habitat: Urban, desert oases — wherever fan palms grow

desc: Large sphinx with scalloped HW edges. Gray-brown. Poplar/willow feeder.

fm.Pattern: Gray-brown with wavy lines; scalloped HW margin
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; adults non-feeding; to lights

desc: Large sphinx with intricate brown/white/yellow pattern. Tropical, reaching LA in warm years.

fm.Pattern: Complex brown/white/yellow mosaic; yellow HW spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful hovering; tropical vagrant/colonizer

desc: Tan noctuid. One of the most costly agricultural pests in the Americas.

fm.Pattern: Tan to olive-brown; dark spot on FW
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; strong migrant; larvae bore into corn ears, tomatoes

desc: Oak underwing — cryptic bark pattern above, orange/black HW revealed in flight.

fm.Pattern: Gray bark-like FW; orange-red HW with black bands
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; rests on oak bark — nearly invisible; flashes bright HW in flight

desc: Tiny cosmopolitan pest of Brassicaceae. Diamond pattern when wings folded.

fm.Pattern: Gray-brown; pale diamond-shapes visible along back when folded
fm.Behavior: Can be extremely abundant; migrant; pesticide-resistant

hp: Brassicaceae: cabbage, kale, broccoli, canola — global pest

desc: Tiny moth whose larvae mine serpentine trails in citrus leaves. Major pest since 2000 arrival in SoCal.

fm.Pattern: White/silver with dark streaks; fringed wings
fm.Behavior: Larvae mine distinctive serpentine trails visible in citrus leaves

desc: Slender plume moth. Wings split. Common on morning glory.

fm.Pattern: Pale tan; wings split into plumes; T-shape at rest
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; rests with wings in T

desc: Larvae build enormous communal silk webs engulfing entire tree branches. White adult moth. Native despite being called a pest.

fm.Pattern: White; sometimes with dark spots
fm.Behavior: Larvae build conspicuous communal web tents on trees
fm.Hosts: Polyphagous — willows, mulberry, walnut, many trees

hp: Polyphagous: 100+ tree species inc. mulberry, walnut, maple

desc: Larvae build silken tents in tree forks — a spring phenomenon. Adults are stocky tan-brown moths.

fm.Pattern: Tan-brown; two pale diagonal FW lines
fm.Behavior: Larvae in communal silk tents in tree forks; spring defoliator

desc: Striking: scarlet FW with gray-blue stripes. Larvae feed on lichens growing on tree bark. Frequently at lights.

fm.Pattern: Scarlet-red FW with gray-blue stripes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; larvae on lichens

hp: Lichens and algae on tree bark — not plant-feeding

desc: Day-flying dark moth with yellow-orange collar. Metallic blue-black wings. Wasp mimic. Visits flowers.

fm.Pattern: Metallic blue-black; yellow-orange collar/neck
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits flowers; wasp mimic

desc: Day-flying buck moth related to H. electra. Desert populations. Stinging caterpillar. Male yellow, female gray-pink.

fm.Pattern: ♂: yellow with black eye spot. ♀: gray-pink.
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; stinging caterpillar

desc: Gray moth. Named for dagger-mark on FW. Caterpillar is yellow with black bristle tufts — spectacular.

fm.Pattern: Gray; dark dagger-shaped FW mark
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; showy caterpillar often more noticed than adult

desc: Large gray sphinx with wavy FW lines. Visits flowers at dusk. Larvae on ash and privet.

fm.Pattern: Gray with wavy dark and pale FW lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hovers at flowers

desc: Streamlined tropical sphinx with horn-like head. Reaches SoCal as summer immigrant.

fm.Pattern: Olive-brown; streamlined; distinctive triangular head
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful migrant from tropics

desc: Huge yellow moth with purple-brown patches. One of largest NA moths. Very rare vagrant to SoCal at extreme western range edge.

fm.Pattern: Yellow with purple-brown patches and spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; adults non-feeding; to lights

desc: Silver comma-mark on FW — like related Autographa species. Larvae on lettuce and celery. Common at lights.

fm.Pattern: Gray-brown; silver comma/falcate mark on FW
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; larvae are loopers

desc: Bark-mimicking geometrid on oak trunks. Cryptic mottled gray-brown. Wingspan variable.

fm.Pattern: Mottled gray-brown; bark-mimicking; variable
fm.Behavior: Rests on oak bark — nearly invisible

desc: Day-flying black-and-white moth. Distinctive pattern. Visits flowers. Larvae on goldenrod.

fm.Pattern: Black with white spots; day-flying
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits flowers; distinctive pattern

desc: Small straw-colored moth. Larvae damage Brassicaceae crops with cross-striped pattern.

fm.Pattern: Straw-yellow with dark cross-lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae are cross-striped on crucifers

desc: Small dark-and-white crambid. Larvae web leaves of beets, spinach, amaranth. Pantropical.

fm.Pattern: Dark brown with white transverse bands
fm.Behavior: Larvae web leaves; common on Amaranthaceae

desc: Striking black moth with white spots. Larvae roll grape leaves. Day-active occasionally.

fm.Pattern: Black with 2 white spots per wing
fm.Behavior: Larvae roll grape/Virginia creeper leaves into tubes

desc: Tiny, pale moth abundant in grass. Often flushed when walking through lawn. One of the most common micro-moths.

fm.Pattern: Pale tan; tiny dark FW spots
fm.Behavior: Flushed from grass when walking; to lights

desc: Medium crambid with distinctive elongated resting posture. Extremely common at lights. Larvae on legumes/grasses.

fm.Pattern: Gray-brown; elongated wings held tight at rest
fm.Behavior: Abundant at lights; rests with wings wrapped tight

desc: Colorful day-resting moth that matches flower heads of host plant perfectly. Green/pink/cream.

fm.Pattern: Green, pink, and cream — matches Asteraceae flower heads
fm.Behavior: Day-resting on flower heads; perfectly camouflaged

desc: Tiny white geometrid. Larvae eat flowers — unusual for a moth. Very common, often unnoticed due to small size.

fm.Pattern: White with faint gray markings; tiny
fm.Behavior: To lights; larvae eat flowers/buds (florivorous)

desc: Small, unusual moth whose caterpillar constructs a portable leaf case. Uncommon family — only a few NA species.

fm.Pattern: Tan-brown with scalloped wing edges
fm.Behavior: Caterpillar builds portable leaf case — carries it while feeding

desc: Tiny orange-and-brown moth similar to P. inornatalis. On native sages and mints. Day-flying.

fm.Pattern: Orange with brown margins
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; flutters near sage flowers

desc: Large silkmoth of mountain pine forests. Periodic outbreaks defoliate pines. Larvae edible — Native American food source.
hp: Pinaceae: Pinus ponderosa, P. jeffreyi, P. contorta

desc: Brown sphinx moth with blue and pink hindwing eyespots. Larvae on cherry and plum.

fm.Wings: Brown sphinx moth with blue and pink hindwing eyespots

desc: Olive and pink sphinx moth. Larvae on Galium (bedstraw) and Epilobium (fireweed).
Small moth with translucent wings. Larvae defoliate coast live oaks. Endemic to California.
hp: Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak) — endemic CA specialist

desc: Small tortricid moth. Pest of citrus and ornamentals. Widespread in urban areas.
hp: Polyphagous: citrus, apple, rose, many ornamentals

desc: Female wingless. Larvae (inchworms) on oaks. Related to eastern spring cankerworm.
Major pest of almonds, walnuts, and citrus. Can carry aflatoxin-producing fungi.
hp: Juglandaceae/Rosaceae: almonds, walnuts, pistachios, figs, citrus

fm.Wings: Major pest of almonds, walnuts, and citrus

desc: Expanding its range westward. Larvae cut bean and corn seedlings at soil level.
Pink hindwings, dark forewings with white veins. Mountain meadows and chaparral.

fm.Color: Dark forewings with white veins; pink hindwings

desc: Pest of beehives — larvae eat beeswax combs. Used extensively as model organism in immunology research.
hp: Apis mellifera: beeswax, pollen, honey — beehive pest

desc: Caterpillar builds portable case from silk and plant debris. Female wingless, never leaves bag.

fm.Wings: Caterpillar builds portable case from silk and plant debris

desc: White moth whose larvae mimic velvet ants with orange and black coloring and faux stinger.

fm.Wings: White moth whose larvae mimic velvet ants with orange and black coloring and faux stinger

desc: Devastating defoliator. Males brown and fly; females white and flightless. Subject of active eradication programs.
hp: Polyphagous: 500+ tree species, esp. oaks, willows, birch

desc: European sphinx moth introduced for biological control of leafy spurge. Colorful caterpillar.
Large gray sphinx moth. Caterpillar is the notorious tomato hornworm with horn at rear.
Wasp-mimicking moth with clear wings. Larva bores into peach and plum trees at ground level.

fm.Color: Clear wings; wasp mimic with blue-black body

desc: Small tortricid moth whose larvae roll grape leaves. Minor vineyard pest.

fm.Wings: Small tortricid moth whose larvae roll grape leaves

desc: Small gray moth — major stone fruit pest. Larvae bore into peach and nectarine twigs and fruit.
Historic cotton pest — first GM crop (Bt cotton) developed to combat it. Now largely eradicated from US.
hp: Malvaceae: cotton (Gossypium) — Bt cotton developed for control

desc: Australian tortricid detected in CA 2007. Quarantine pest of many crops. Subject of eradication efforts.
hp: Polyphagous: apple, grape, citrus, many crops and ornamentals

desc: Brown geometrid moth whose inchworm larvae feed on juniper foliage.

fm.Wings: Brown geometrid moth whose inchworm larvae feed on juniper foliage

desc: Small orange moth with white-spotted wings. Larvae web tree-of-heaven leaves. Expanding with host tree.

fm.Wings: Small orange moth with white-spotted wings

desc: T-shaped moth with feathery wings. Larvae bore into artichoke heads. Specialized pest.
Green-gray noctuid. Larvae cut plant stems at soil level. Overwinters as adult.
White prominent moth. Caterpillars bright green with bizarre forked tail — defensive display.
Dark noctuid moth. Larvae sever seedlings at soil line — major agricultural pest. Migratory.
hp: Polyphagous: seedlings of many crops and garden plants

desc: Yellow moth with dark spots. Caterpillars covered in tufts of black and yellow hair on willows.
Gray prominent moth. Larvae gregarious on oaks — can defoliate trees during outbreaks.
hp: Fagaceae: oaks (Quercus) — can defoliate during outbreaks

desc: Brown geometrid moth. Larvae (inchworms) on conifers. Periodic defoliating outbreaks.
Brown noctuid migrating to mountains in summer. Called miller moth when abundant. Important food for grizzly bears.
hp: Polyphagous: alfalfa, wheat, many crops and grasses

fm.Habitat: Agricultural areas; mountain summits in summer

desc: Metallic blue day-flying wasp-mimic moth with orange head. Larvae on grasses. Striking appearance.
hp: Poaceae: grasses — larvae feed on grass blades

fm.Color: Metallic blue body; dark wings; orange head

desc: Small tan geometrid. Inchworm larvae on many trees. Common at lights.
Gray-brown noctuid. Larvae devastating to corn, grasses. Migratory — cannot overwinter in cold areas.
hp: Polyphagous: corn, grasses, sorghum, rice, many crops

desc: Gray noctuid. Larvae with yellow and black stripes — serious pest of vegetables and field crops in West.
hp: Polyphagous: alfalfa, sugar beets, tomato, many crops

desc: Brown noctuid on cotton family plants. Increasingly common with climate warming.
Green sphinx moth with dark band. Desert and subtropical. At northern range edge in LA County.
Close relative of M. californicum. Communal tent-building caterpillars on desert willows and other shrubs.
hp: Rosaceae, Salicaceae: willows, wild rose, mountain mahogany

desc: Brown noctuid. Major soybean pest in warmer regions. Migratory — reaches SoCal in warm years.
Small gray pyralid. Larvae bore into mesquite seed pods. Desert species reaching LA County margins.
Caterpillars march in processions. Urticating hairs cause severe dermatitis. European — under surveillance in CA.
Very common small tan geometrid at lights. Inchworm larvae on many plants.
Large reddish-brown silkmoth. Western relative of cecropia. Larvae on willow, cherry, mountain mahogany.
hp: Rosaceae: cherry, plum; Salicaceae: willow; Cercocarpus

desc: Small brown moth of desert edges. Larvae on various low plants.
Mottled gray moth with letter-like markings. Larvae on Rubus (blackberry). Hook-tip moth relative.
Brown noctuid with curved lines and pink tinge. Larvae on many herbaceous plants.
Fuzzy gray-brown moth resembling a small piece of bark. Larvae on cherry, ash. Interesting silk-moth relative.
Tiny T-shaped plume moth. Larvae on snapdragon and toadflax. Distinctive rolled wings at rest give all plume moths their feathery appearance.
Pale plume moth on yarrow (Achillea). Found mainland and Catalina Island. Larvae bore into yarrow stems and flower heads.
White plume moth associated with Baccharis (coyote brush). Malibu specimen records. Larvae feed on coyote brush flower heads.
Tiny introduced moth with distinctive yellow V mark on dark wings. Larvae damage wine corks and dried foods. Originally from Mediterranean.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with yellow V mark

desc: Stout reddish-brown moth. Larvae build conspicuous silk tents in tree forks — a familiar spring sight on oaks and willows. Cyclic population booms.

fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, riparian — communal larval tents in tree forks

desc: Small bright green sphinx moth of coastal sage scrub. Day-flying — hovers at flowers. Green fades to yellow in preserved specimens. California near-endemic.

fm.Color: Bright green FW (fades in collection); dark HW

desc: Males gray with faint crossbands; females wingless. Larvae are common 'inchworms' on deciduous trees. Emerge after first fall rains.

fm.Color: ♂ gray with faint bands; ♀ wingless, gray-brown

desc: Small tan geometrid. Like fall cankerworm, ♀ is wingless. Larvae feed on oak and other hardwoods. Can outbreak and defoliate.
Small pale geometrid with fine dark speckling. Larvae on currants and gooseberries. Common at lights throughout LA suburbs.

fm.Color: Pale cream-yellow with fine brown speckling

desc: Small narrow moth that rests with wings wrapped around body like a tube. Larvae damage turf grass. The moth you flush from lawns when walking at dusk.

fm.Habitat: Turf grass, lawns — larvae damage roots

desc: Small olive-brown crambid with wavy pale crosslines. Larvae web together alfalfa and beet leaves. Very common at lights in agricultural areas.
Dull brown concealer moth found indoors. Larvae eat wool, fur, feathers, dried food, and even book bindings. Originally from Asia.
Variable brown noctuid with dark C-mark on forewing. Larvae are common garden cutworms. One of the most frequently photographed moths at LA blacklights.

fm.Color: Brown with dark C/dagger mark on FW

desc: Pale gray geometrid with wavy crosslines. Very common at lights. Larvae on many hardwoods. One of the 'LBMs' (little brown moths) that dominate LA blacklight sheets.

fm.Color: Pale gray with wavy dark crosslines

desc: Small bright yellow geometrid with brown FW border. Day-flying — frequents flowers. One of the few geometrids active by day. Larvae on honey locust.

fm.Color: Bright yellow with brown FW border

desc: Pink-and-yellow geometrid — strikingly colorful for a moth. Larvae feed on chickweed and clover. Common at lights. The 'cherry blossom moth.'

fm.Color: Pink bands on yellow ground — very colorful

desc: Large yellow-tan geometrid with pointed wing tips. Single dark line across both wings. Very common at lights. Larvae on many trees.

fm.Color: Yellow-tan; single dark diagonal crossline; pointed FW tips

desc: Black forewings with cream lines; orange-pink hindwings with black spots. Classic tiger moth pattern. Larvae ('woolly bears') feed on many low plants.

fm.Color: Black FW with cream lines; orange-pink HW with black spots
fm.Habitat: Grassland, chaparral — larvae on low plants

desc: Small tan crambid with dark markings. Larvae web together leaves of beans, beets, and many garden plants. Very common at lights.

fm.Color: Tan with dark brown spots and lines
fm.Habitat: Vegetable gardens — larvae web leaves together

desc: Tiny gold-tan moth. The classic fabric-eating clothes moth — larvae spin silken tubes in wool, fur, feathers. Weak flier that avoids light (unlike pantry moths).

fm.Habitat: Closets — larvae eat wool, silk, fur, feathers
fm.Behavior: Avoids light (unlike pantry moths); runs rather than flies

desc: Similar to webbing clothes moth but with three dark spots on each FW. Larvae build portable silk cases. Damages woolens, furs, and taxidermy.

fm.Color: Brown-tan with 3 dark FW spots (vs. plain in webbing moth)
fm.Habitat: Closets, museums — larvae in portable silk cases

desc: Pale green-white with brown patch and white dot. Larvae spectacular — green with yellow side stripe on oak. Classic prominent moth shape at rest (tented wings).

fm.Color: Pale green-white; brown FW patch with white dot

desc: Dark noctuid with pale crossband. Larvae are striking — velvety black with yellow lateral stripes. Pest of ornamentals and vegetables.

fm.Color: Dark brown with pale median band
fm.Habitat: Gardens — larvae velvety black with yellow stripes

desc: Cryptic bark-pattern forewings conceal brilliant orange-and-black hindwings — revealed in flash-and-vanish startle display. Larvae on oak. One of several CA Catocala species.

fm.Color: Bark-pattern FW; brilliant orange + black HW
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland — cryptic at rest on bark
fm.Behavior: Flash-and-vanish: reveals bright HW when startled

desc: Tiny straw-colored moth that rests with wings wrapped tightly around body. Extremely common in lawns — the moth flushed by every footstep at dusk.

fm.Habitat: Turf grass — extremely common in lawns
fm.Behavior: Flushed en masse when walking through grass at dusk

desc: Spectacular wasp mimic with clear wings — one of the most convincing mimicry systems in insects. Larvae bore in sycamore. Day-flying. Often mistaken for a yellowjacket.

fm.Behavior: Day-flying wasp mimic; remarkably convincing

desc: Large heavy-bodied moth with mottled bark-pattern wings. Larvae bore 1-inch tunnels through living hardwood trunks. 3-year larval cycle. Can damage structural trees.

fm.Habitat: Hardwood trees — larvae bore large tunnels in trunk

desc: Dark mottled cutworm moth. Larvae feed on roots and stems of many garden plants. Very common at blacklights. One of the most frequently encountered noctuids in urban LA.

fm.Color: Dark brown mottled; pale reniform spot

desc: Pale tan-brown with single white dot on each FW. Larvae march in armies across fields (hence name). Major pest of turf and grain crops. Migratory from Mexico.

fm.Color: Pale tan-brown; single white dot on each FW
fm.Habitat: Turf, grain — larvae march in groups

desc: Small gray-brown noctuid with kidney-shaped (reniform) spot. Very common at lights. Larvae feed on clover, alfalfa, and many garden plants.
Small triangular moth with pointed snout (labial palps). Larvae are green 'clover worms' on legumes. Very common at lights — one of the first moths seen at any LA blacklight.

fm.Color: Brown; pointed snout; triangular at rest

desc: Small gray-brown litter moth with wavy crosslines. Larvae feed on dead leaves and decaying wood. Among the most numerically abundant moths at blacklights.

fm.Habitat: Woodland — larvae in leaf litter and dead wood

desc: Small dark brown owlet with slanted crosslines. Feeds on dead leaves and fungi on the forest floor. Common at lights in foothill oak woodland.
Small pale geometrid with sharp-angled crosslines. Very common at lights spring through fall. Larvae on many hardwoods. Subtle but frequent.

fm.Color: Pale tan with sharp dark crosslines at angle

desc: Delicate pale cream geometrid with scalloped brown wing borders. One of the most elegant small moths. Larvae on dandelion, chickweed. Common in gardens.

fm.Color: Pale cream with scalloped brown borders — lacy appearance
fm.Habitat: Gardens, weedy areas — common at lights

desc: Small brown crambid with translucent wing patches. Larvae web leaves of beets, chard, spinach. Pantropical species — common in SoCal gardens.

fm.Color: Brown with translucent patches and dark spots

desc: Tiny gray-brown moth. Larvae mine potato tubers and tomato/eggplant leaves. Major stored-potato pest worldwide. Common in LA home gardens and warehouses.

fm.Color: Narrow gray-brown wings with dark speckling
fm.Habitat: Potato, tomato, eggplant — mines tubers and leaves

desc: Pale tan dart. Larvae cut grain roots underground. Serious western agriculture pest.
Tiny noctuid perfectly mimicking bird droppings — white-and-brown splotch. Remarkable camouflage on leaves.
Gray-brown dart with pinkish reniform spot. Common at blacklights. Cutworm larvae damage gardens.
Pale straw with dark vein dots. Larvae on grasses. Common at lights.
Striking white FW edged olive-brown and lilac. Resembles bird dropping or lichen. Larvae on grape.
Tan-brown with dark streaks. Larvae gregarious — black-yellow caterpillars arch when disturbed.
Small emerald-green geometrid. Larvae decorate themselves with flower petals — living camouflage.
Pale gray with sharp dark bent crossline. Very common at lights. Subtle but frequent.
Small olive-brown crambid. Larvae tie celery and beet leaves. Very common at lights year-round.
Small gray pyralid. Major flour mill pest worldwide. Larvae contaminate flour with silk.
Small gray pyralid. Major California dried fruit pest (raisins, dates, figs).
Small brown bell-shaped tortricid. Larvae roll leaves of grape, citrus, 100+ plants.
Variable tan tortricid. Larvae damage apple, cherry, citrus fruit and leaves.
Pale gray sphinx. Larvae on snowberry. Mountain chaparral. Clean elegant appearance.
Spectacular wasp mimic — metallic green FW, orange HW and legs. Larvae bore squash vine stems. Day-flying.
Small dark erebid. Larvae on sumac (Rhus). Very common at lights in chaparral.
Small white moth. Unlike true yucca moth, does not pollinate — cheater in the yucca-moth mutualism.
Tiny variable noctuid — one of the most abundant small moths at lights. Green, brown, or gray forms. Larvae on grasses.
Brown noctuid with woodgrain pattern. Spring species — among the first moths at lights each year. Larvae on many hardwoods.
Gray-brown dart. Larvae are cutworms. Common fall species at blacklights in LA.
Tiny moth with pink-and-olive pattern. Despite small size, beautifully marked. Larvae on grasses.

fm.Color: Pink and olive bands — colorful for tiny moth

desc: Small noctuid with distinctive pink median band across gray wings. Larvae on ragweed.
Pale whitish dart. Desert species common at eastern LA County blacklights. Larvae on low desert plants.
Rich russet-orange noctuid. Fall-flying. Larvae on oak. One of the more colorful noctuids.
Large brown noctuid with coppery hindwings. Cryptic bark pattern. Common at lights and bait traps fall.
Gray-brown with silver U mark on FW. Pest of lettuce, beans, and brassicas. Hovers at flowers at dusk.
Dark brown dart with pinkish tinge. Larvae damage vegetables and cotton underground. Year-round in SoCal.
Small gray-brown noctuid with subtle mottled pattern. Introduced from Europe. Larvae on low plants. Very common at lights.
Bright emerald green with white crosslines. One of several green geometrids. Larvae on oaks.
Large gray geometrid with bark-like pattern. Dramatic — one of the largest geometrids.
Males tan with dark crosslines; females wingless. Late fall species — emerges after first cold snap.
Olive-green noctuid with pink flush. Larvae exclusively on ferns. Beautiful subtle moth.
Famous 'industrial melanism' textbook moth. Pale with dark speckling. Classic evolutionary biology example.

fm.Color: White peppered with black — textbook evolution

desc: Gray with dark crosslines and bands. Very common at lights spring-fall. Variable pattern.
Tiny gray pug moth with four dark discal spots. Pugs are a huge genus — this is the commonest in LA.
Dark noctuid with kidney-shaped mark. Major worldwide pest of brassicas. Larvae bore into cabbage hearts.
Pale tan with dark spots and streaks. Spectacular hairy caterpillars on sycamore. Common in LA riparian areas.
Cream-tan with checkered pattern. Larvae spectacularly hairy. Common at lights summer-fall.
Gray with dark dagger marks. Larvae are green with red spots on oak. Fall species.
Distinctive scalloped hindwings. Desert/foothill noctuid. Larvae on low desert plants.
Gray with pointed hood-like thorax. Larvae spectacularly striped on asters and goldenrod.
White and gray with sharp dark crosslines. Attractive contrast pattern. Larvae on birch and alder.
Small pure white crambid. Very common at lights. Larvae in grass thatch.
Brown crambid with translucent patches. Very common at lights spring-fall. Larvae on grasses.
Pale straw crambid. Larvae damage carrot and parsley seed heads. Common in herb gardens.
Tiny brown-tan gelechiid. Larvae skeletonize leaves of many deciduous trees. Very common but tiny.
Tan-brown prominent. Gregarious caterpillars on walnut and hickory — arch heads and tails when disturbed.
Small dark moth with ruby-red hindwings. Woolly bear caterpillar. Flies early in season. Common.
Pure white with small black dots. Bright orange-yellow abdomen. Similar to Virginia tiger moth. Common at lights.

fm.Color: White with black dots; orange abdomen

desc: Pale with pink crosslines. Larvae on willow. Delicate and attractive. Common at riparian lights.
Pale with single sharp dark crossline from apex. Larvae on knotweed and docks. Elegant minimalist pattern.
Large bright yellow geometrid — looks like a dead leaf. Notched wing margins. Larvae on maple, oak. Fall.
Small gray pyralid. Larvae bore into legume seed pods — lima beans, soybeans. Cosmopolitan pest.
Tiny moth with white head and shoulders. Very common indoors. Larvae eat plant debris, dried food, fabrics.
Small furry moth. Larvae ('asps') are extremely venomous — covered with urticating hairs causing intense pain. Rare but medically important.

fm.Warning: Larvae extremely venomous — intense pain from hairs

desc: Emerald geometrid — bright green geometrid moth with delicate white crosslines. One of the most beautiful small moths. Fades to pale after death. Larvae on legumes.

fm.Color: Bright emerald green with white crosslines

desc: Pale litter moth — small gray-brown erebid of leaf litter habitats. Larvae on dead leaves and detritus. Very common at lights but easily overlooked.
Filbertworm moth — small tortricid whose larvae bore into acorns, filberts, and chestnuts. Important nut pest. Bell-shaped at rest with coppery wing markings.
Mexican tiger moth — black forewings with cream stripes; orange-red hindwings with black spots. Classic tiger moth pattern. Woolly bear caterpillars on low plants.

fm.Color: Black FW + cream stripes; orange-red HW + spots

desc: Six-banded choreutid — tiny metallic moth with six dark bands. Day-flying. Larvae mine in mullein leaves. Beautiful under magnification.

fm.Color: Metallic with six dark bands; day-flying

desc: Dark-eyed noctuid — small gray-brown noctuid with distinctive dark eye mark on forewing. Common at lights in chaparral habitat.
Lunate zale — large gray noctuid with scalloped wing margins and lunate (crescent) marks. Cryptic bark-pattern. Common at lights and bait traps in oak woodland.

fm.Color: Gray with scalloped margins; crescent marks

desc: Ruddy dart — gray-brown noctuid with reddish tinge. Fall-flying. Common at blacklights in foothill areas.
Willow prominent — gray prominent moth with bark-like pattern. Larvae on willow. Tented wings at rest give distinctive profile.

fm.Color: Gray bark-pattern; tented wings at rest

desc: Green cutworm moth — pale green-gray noctuid. Larvae are cutworms on clover and alfalfa. Common at lights spring-fall.
Togated catocala — brown underwing moth with marked FW. Common at lights in oak woodland and chaparral. Larvae on various shrubs.
Coral bean moth — brown crambid with intricate pattern. Larvae bore into coral bean (Erythrina) seeds and stems. Common wherever coral trees grow.
Slender emerald — delicate pale green geometrid with faint crosslines. One of several emerald moths in LA County. Larvae on various trees.
Divergent underwing — gray noctuid with orange-red hindwings. Flash-and-vanish display. Common in desert and foothill habitats.
Irrorated geometrid — pale gray geometrid peppered with dark scales. Very common at lights. Larvae on many shrubs.

fm.Color: Pale gray peppered with dark scales

desc: Half-marked oecophorid — small brown concealer moth introduced from Australia. Larvae feed on dead eucalyptus leaves. Very common wherever eucalyptus grows.
Dangerous crambid — brown crambid moth found in chaparral and woodland. Larvae web leaves of various shrubs. Common at lights.
